news.title: Wallabies vs Ireland live: Captain’s try scrubbed out in contentious obstruction call

sections.heading: Logistics and revenue drive Fiji's UK residency
sections.body: Fiji's decision to play their home Nations Championship fixtures in the UK represents a massive financial windfall. Unable to host games in Suva due to the tournament's 25,000-capacity minimum, the union has embraced the logistical shift. "It’s a transformational opportunity for us," explained Fiji Rugby chairman John Sanday. The revenue generated will fund local high-performance facilities.

sections.heading: Fijians shift tactical focus to set-piece stability
sections.body: While today's coverage contains no injury updates from the squad's UK training camp, the players have shifted their tactical focus toward set-piece stability. Rectifying the defensive organization that crumbled in the 8-73 defeat against England remains the absolute priority. Staff are driving high-intensity conditioning sessions to ensure the team can physically match their next opponents in Edinburgh.

sections.heading: Injuries deplete Scotland ahead of Edinburgh clash
sections.body: Scotland have suffered a major triple injury blow with three Glasgow Warriors ruled out of the upcoming match. Finisher Kyle Steyn, loose forward Rory Darge, and lock Alex Samuel will all miss out. Despite these absences, Gregor Townsend has not called up any replacements. The hosts expect a fierce backlash from the visitors after their heavy defeat last weekend.

sections.heading: Fiji seek redemption from bottom of the table
sections.body: Fiji goes into the weekend propping up the Nations Championship standings in 12th place, desperately needing a positive result to kickstart their campaign. Back-to-back defeats leave them with a point differential of minus 80. Avoiding another heavy loss is crucial for rebuilding confidence. A resilient performance in Edinburgh would finally move the team in the right direction.

sections.heading: Quirke joins squad as scrum-half battle intensifies
sections.body: Raffi Quirke has joined the squad to replace Alex Mitchell ahead of the trip to Santiago del Estero. The Newcastle Red Bulls signing, who debuted in 2021, joins Jack van Poortvliet and Ben Spencer in a three-way fight for the No. 9 shirt. Steve Borthwick also faces external pressure to start Henry Pollock following the youngster’s hat-trick during the 73-8 victory over Fiji.

sections.heading: Mitchell sidelined by hamstring strain
sections.body: Alex Mitchell has been ruled out of Saturday’s Test after sustaining a hamstring injury during the win against Fiji. The scrum-half was forced off in Liverpool and will play no part in the final match of the summer tour. No other fresh injury concerns were reported in today’s coverage, with the remaining squad members focusing on conditioning and recovery before flying to Argentina.

sections.heading: Contepomi fuels tension ahead of Santiago clash
sections.body: Felipe Contepomi has reignited a feud with Tom Curry, labeling the flanker a “bully” following a previous encounter at Allianz Stadium. The Argentina head coach remains aggrieved by a tackle on Juan Cruz Mallía, ensuring a hostile reception at the Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades. Jamie George expects a football-style atmosphere in a city already heightened by Argentina’s progress in the football World Cup.

sections.heading: England seek mid-table climb in Round 3
sections.body: England go into the weekend seventh in the standings following their record-breaking 11-try performance against Fiji. With one win and one loss from the opening two rounds, a victory in Santiago del Estero is essential to keep pace with the log leaders. Success on the road would secure a positive return for the summer tour before the championship resumes in the autumn.

match.venue: Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades, Santiago del Estero

sections.heading: Momentum builds after clinical Brisbane win
sections.body: Les Bleus arrive in the final week of the July window with momentum after dispatching Australia 42-26 in Round two. Following the opening defeat to New Zealand, the clinical performance in Brisbane has restored confidence. Fabien Galthié's squad is now focused on tactical execution before the trip to face Japan, seeking a second consecutive win to climb the inaugural championship standings.

sections.heading: Penaud remains unavailable for selection
sections.body: Damian Penaud remains the significant absentee for the match against Japan. The winger was ruled out of the remainder of the tournament following a calf injury sustained during the Round one defeat to New Zealand. With no new fitness concerns reported following the physical victory over the Wallabies, the coaching staff is prioritizing recovery protocols and tactical conditioning during the current training block.

sections.heading: Japan look to recapture giant-killing form
sections.body: Japan enter the contest as underdogs but remain a threat through their high-tempo style and lack of fear against Tier One nations. While France secured a dominant 52-12 victory the last time these sides met in Paris, the Brave Blossoms have a history of upsetting established powers — Les Bleus will need to maintain defensive discipline to nullify Japan's ability to create chaos in transition.

sections.heading: Bonus point vital for top-four status
sections.body: France go into the weekend fourth in the log after recovering from their opening loss. With a record of one win and one defeat, securing a bonus-point victory is essential to keep pressure on the leading pack. Given the tight margins in the mid-table, any slip in clinical finishing could see France overtaken by chasing sides before the tournament concludes its July phase.

sections.heading: Selection dilemmas as Baloucoune returns to training
sections.body: Andy Farrell faces a welcome selection headache on the wings following the return of Rob Baloucoune to full training in Auckland. The Ulster winger missed the opening wins against Australia and Japan with a hamstring issue but is now in contention. With Jimmy O’Brien also showing form, assistant coach Andrew Goodman says: "It creates a healthy dilemma for us as coaches and some good discussions."

sections.heading: Full squad available for Auckland series finale
sections.body: Ireland are reporting a clean bill of health as they prepare for the tour's final Test. All 36 members of the travelling squad took part in Tuesday’s session at Kings College, including prop Jeremy Loughman. Loughman has completed his return-to-play protocols after sitting out the Japan victory due to a head injury. The group shows no signs of fatigue despite entering their 12th Test of the campaign.

sections.heading: All Blacks defend 52-game Eden Park unbeaten streak
sections.body: New Zealand enter the weekend unbeaten in the Nations Championship after securing victories over France and Italy. Head coach Dave Rennie acknowledges Ireland’s ability, particularly given their 2022 series win on Kiwi soil. The All Blacks have not lost at their Auckland fortress in 32 years, a run spanning 52 matches. Goodman has identified the need to specifically target the way the hosts try to play.

sections.heading: Ireland hunt historic win to maintain title charge
sections.body: Ireland go into the weekend third in the log following back-to-back bonus point victories. A first win at Eden Park since 1994 would keep Farrell’s side firmly in the hunt for the inaugural title — keeping pace with the tournament leaders. With both sides currently unbeaten, the result in Auckland will likely dictate which side carries the most momentum into the tournament's final phase in November.

sections.heading: Cannone suspended for four matches
sections.body: Lock forward Niccolò Cannone will miss the remainder of the Nations Championship tour after receiving a four-match ban on Tuesday. The suspension follows a red card issued during the Round 2 defeat to New Zealand. His absence leaves a significant hole in the engine room as Italy seeks to rebound from consecutive losses and replicate their recent success against the Wallabies.

sections.heading: Engine room depth tested in Perth
sections.body: With Niccolò Cannone ruled out following his disciplinary hearing, the Azzurri medical and coaching staff have shifted focus to high-intensity conditioning drills in Perth. No fresh injury concerns emerged from today's coverage, allowing the squad to prioritize set-piece cohesion. The pack remains largely intact from the Wellington fixture, though the second-row rotation will require adjustment before the weekend.

sections.heading: Schmidt wary of Wallaby slump
sections.body: Australia enters the weekend having lost nine of their last 10 Tests, a run of form that has Joe Schmidt on high alert. While the Wallabies are monitoring a key unnamed playmaker in a race to be fit, they remain vulnerable. Italy’s 26-19 victory over Australia in November 2025, fueled by tries from Louis Lynagh and Monty Ioane, provides a psychological blueprint for an upset.

sections.heading: Escaping the basement in Perth
sections.body: Italy goes into the weekend sitting 11 of 12 on the log, needing a victory to avoid being rooted to the bottom of the table. After a heavy loss to the All Blacks, a result in Perth would represent a significant turnaround. A win would not only provide the first points of the campaign but also reinforce Italy’s recent edge over a struggling Australian side.

sections.heading: Townsend eyes clinical finish
sections.body: Following the narrow defeat to South Africa, Scotland are recalibrating their attack for Saturday’s visit of Fiji. Forwards coach John Dalziel noted that while the team is creating numerous opportunities, they must be more clinical in the red zone. Dalziel emphasized that the squad is no longer satisfied with competitive performances alone, stating, “We want to win these games. We’ve still got that last bit of growing to do.”

sections.heading: Triple blow for home side
sections.body: Scotland’s preparations have been hindered by a triple injury setback. Kyle Steyn is sidelined with a foot knock, while Rory Darge remains unavailable following the concussion he sustained during the interval against South Africa. Furthermore, Alex Samuel is set for knee surgery and will be out for several weeks. Gregor Townsend has opted not to call up any replacements to the squad at this stage.

sections.heading: Fiji bracing for response
sections.body: Fiji are currently based in the UK for their Nations Championship fixtures due to home stadium capacity requirements. Following a heavy defeat to England, Scotland’s coaching staff are anticipating a significant "backlash" from the visitors this weekend. The Fijian side, who prioritize these fixtures to secure vital revenue, are known for their physical intensity and will be looking to disrupt Scotland’s momentum at Murrayfield.

sections.heading: Seeking a points boost
sections.body: Scotland currently sit 6th in the log with 4 points from two matches. With a point difference of -5, Gregor Townsend’s side are aiming to climb the table before the weekend’s conclusion. A victory on Saturday is essential for Scotland to maintain their trajectory and build upon the lessons learned in Pretoria, as they strive to bridge the gap toward the tournament’s elite contenders.

sections.heading: Squad additions confirmed
sections.body: Wales have added forward Freddie Thomas and hooker Elliot Dee to the squad ahead of the fixture in Durban. The group is preparing for the challenge of facing South Africa, with forwards coach Danny Wilson stating that the team is relishing the opportunity to play against the world champions this coming weekend.

sections.heading: Captaincy under review
sections.body: The squad is currently monitoring the fitness of captain Dewi Lake. No further injury updates were provided in the latest reports regarding the wider group. Players are continuing their conditioning work as they focus on tactical preparations for the upcoming round of the Nations Championship.

sections.heading: Springboks name four debutants
sections.body: South Africa enters the weekend with a perfect record in the tournament. Head coach Rassie Erasmus has made 10 changes to the starting side, including the selection of four debutants. Sharks fly-half Vusi Moyo is set to make his international debut, with Pieter-Steph du Toit captaining the Springboks for the match in Durban.

sections.heading: Points table update
sections.body: Wales sits 8th in the standings after two rounds of the competition, following a win and a loss. A victory in Durban would be significant for the side following their recent defeat to Argentina. The squad aims to improve their position on the log as the tournament nears the halfway point.

sections.heading: Contepomi prepares for fiery encounter
sections.body: The Pumas are preparing for a hostile atmosphere in Santiago del Estero as they ready themselves for England. Head coach Felipe Contepomi’s previous criticism of England flanker Tom Curry adds a layer of intensity to the build-up. Contepomi previously labeled Curry a “bully” following an incident in a past encounter, noting, “If we want bullies in this game, good on them.”

sections.heading: Status remains unconfirmed
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news regarding the Argentina squad in today’s coverage. Without specific reports on player availability or individual fitness concerns from the camp, the focus remains on the upcoming tactical preparation for the visit of the English side. Coaches continue to manage the group’s workload as they approach the final fixture of this Nations Championship block.

sections.heading: England shake up squad for Santiago
sections.body: England have added scrum-half Raffi Quirke to their squad following a hamstring injury to Alex Mitchell. Meanwhile, pressure is mounting on coach Steve Borthwick to start Henry Pollock after his recent performance against Fiji. England captain Jamie George noted the challenge awaiting them, stating, “It’s a challenging place to go,” describing the atmosphere in Argentina as “hostile” and “different” from other venues.

sections.heading: Pumas look to climb the table
sections.body: Argentina enters this round sitting 5th in the standings with 1 win from their opening 2 fixtures. A victory on Saturday is essential to improve upon their current points difference of +5 and climb higher in the log. With the tournament tightly contested, the result at the Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades will be pivotal in determining the final mid-table rankings this weekend.

match.venue: Estadio Único Madre de Ciudades, Santiago del Estero

sections.heading: Schmidt resists veteran recall for crucial Perth Test
sections.body: Coach Joe Schmidt has opted against drafting in seasoned playmakers for the upcoming match against Italy. Despite the struggles of the inexperienced halves during the recent loss to France, the coaching staff has stuck with their current squad. Meanwhile, there is a race to be fit for a key playmaker, providing a potential late boost for the team ahead of this must-win fixture.

sections.heading: Playmaker availability remains the primary focus
sections.body: The squad is currently navigating uncertainty surrounding the fitness of a key playmaker. Aside from the ongoing assessment of this specific player, there is no further information regarding the physical status of the wider group. The coaching staff is balancing the need for selection stability against the requirement for a high-intensity performance to improve upon recent results in this ongoing campaign.

sections.heading: Italy faces challenge following disciplinary setback
sections.body: Italy will be without second row Niccolò Cannone for the clash in Perth after he received a four-match ban for a red card during their recent defeat to the All Blacks. The Azzurri, who defeated Australia in their previous encounter last year, remain a threat that has the coaching staff on edge after the Italians showed considerable potential in their weekend performance against New Zealand.

sections.heading: Wallabies look to arrest slide from bottom half
sections.body: Australia sits 10th on the Nations Championship log after recording losses in both opening rounds. With a points difference of -18, the team is under pressure to secure a result this weekend. A victory is essential to improve their standing as they look to recover from a difficult run that has seen the side lose nine of their past 10 Test matches.

sections.heading: Squad rotation expected after Ireland defeat
sections.body: Following the 20-36 loss to Ireland last weekend, Japan management is expected to review personnel options for the upcoming fixture. Tries from Taira and Hayate Era highlighted Japan’s attacking intent in Newcastle, though the team will look for improved consistency across the eighty minutes. Final selection decisions are expected as the squad refines its tactical approach to close the gap on tournament heavyweights.

sections.heading: Camp focuses on recovery and conditioning
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage following the conclusion of the second round. The team has shifted focus toward intensive conditioning sessions to ensure the group remains physically prepared for the demands of facing France. Coaches are monitoring player loads closely as they finalize preparations for the weekend, maintaining a consistent training rhythm during this short turnaround period.

sections.heading: France arrive after securing away victory
sections.body: France travel to this fixture following a 42-26 win over Australia. The French side displayed high-scoring form, with tries from Emmanuel Meafou, Aaron Grandidier Nkanang, Romain Ntamack, Florian Verhaeghe, and Theo Attissogbe. However, France will be without winger Damian Penaud for this match after he sustained a calf injury during the team's opening defeat against New Zealand earlier in the tournament.

sections.heading: Japan look to climb the table
sections.body: Japan sit ninth on the log after two rounds, holding one win and one loss with a point difference of +1. Having secured 47 points scored against 46 conceded, the squad aims to regain momentum against France. A positive result this weekend is vital for Japan’s aspirations to improve their standing as the mid-table battle in the Nations Championship remains highly competitive.

sections.heading: Rennie prepares for visitors as Ireland reach full strength
sections.body: New Zealand head coach Dave Rennie confirmed his side is well aware of Ireland’s ability as preparation continues for Saturday’s showdown in Auckland. While the All Blacks coaching group focuses on tactical progression, Ireland assistant coach Andrew Goodman has confirmed that visitors are training with a fully fit 36-man squad. Both teams enter the weekend unbeaten in the inaugural Nations Championship competition.

sections.heading: Clean bill of health reported in Ireland's training camp
sections.body: Ireland have received a significant boost with both winger Rob Baloucoune and prop Jeremy Loughman returning to full training this week. Both players are now in selection contention for Saturday’s Test after overcoming hamstring and head injuries respectively. With no fresh injury news emerging from the All Blacks camp, the focus remains on high-intensity training sessions as the squad nears the final selection.

sections.heading: Touring side looks to dismantle Eden Park’s long-standing streak
sections.body: Ireland head into this fixture with momentum, having secured victories over Australia and Japan in their opening Nations Championship matches. Assistant coach Andrew Goodman noted that the squad is embracing the challenge of facing the All Blacks at Eden Park, where the hosts have remained unbeaten in 52 games since 1994. The visitors are viewing this as a pivotal test of their current performance level.

sections.heading: Unbeaten records on the line in top-of-table clash
sections.body: New Zealand enters the weekend second on the log, carrying a perfect record from their opening two matches with 81 points scored and a +32 differential. A win against Ireland would be vital for maintaining pressure at the top of the table. With both sides currently undefeated, the outcome in Auckland will prove decisive in shaping the standings for the remainder of the championship.

sections.heading: Debutant Vusi Moyo leads new faces
sections.body: Sharks fly-half Vusi Moyo is set to make his international debut this weekend as part of a Springbok squad featuring four new caps. Head coach Rassie Erasmus has opted for a mix of established leadership and fresh talent for the encounter in Durban, with Pieter-Steph du Toit retained as captain. The selection also includes a notable return for a World Cup winner following a three-year absence.

sections.heading: Squad focus on tactical preparation
sections.body: There is no fresh injury news in today's coverage regarding the South African camp. The team has maintained a high-intensity focus on the training paddock, concentrating on tactical alignment and physical conditioning ahead of the third-round fixture. The coaching staff remains committed to ensuring the squad is fully prepared for the specific challenges posed by the touring Welsh side this weekend.

sections.heading: Wales face injury concerns after Argentina defeat
sections.body: Wales are managing significant personnel issues following their recent defeat to Argentina. Reports indicate concerns around skipper Dewi Lake, prompting the touring side to call up hooker Elliot Dee and forward Freddie Thomas as replacements. Forwards coach Danny Wilson insists the squad is relishing the upcoming challenge, though they face an uphill task to improve their defensive structure against the world champions in Durban.

sections.heading: Springboks aim to extend winning streak
sections.body: South Africa enters the third round as one of only three teams maintaining a perfect record in the tournament. With two wins from two, the Boks currently sit at the top of the log standings. A successful result on home soil this weekend would reinforce their position at the summit of the table and maintain their momentum in this year’s Nations Championship campaign.
